instruction: Explain TCP congestion control and why it matters for application performance.
output:
<think>
TCP congestion control prevents network collapse by adjusting send rate based on network feedback. Key mechanisms: slow start (exponential growth), congestion avoidance (linear growth), fast retransmit (triple duplicate ACK), fast recovery.

This matters for apps because: new connections start slow (slow start), packet loss causes throughput drops, and high-latency links have lower throughput (bandwidth-delay product).

This is a fundamental concept that appears frequently in software engineering interviews and real-world system design. Understanding the underlying mechanics helps make better architectural decisions and debug performance issues more effectively. The key is to build intuition through practice with concrete examples rather than memorizing abstract definitions.

instruction: Compare gRPC vs REST for different use cases. When is each appropriate?
output:
<think>
REST: text-based (JSON), HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2, request-response pattern, browser-friendly, great tooling. gRPC: binary (Protocol Buffers), HTTP/2, supports streaming, code generation, more efficient.

Use REST for: public APIs, browser clients, simple CRUD. Use gRPC for: internal microservice communication, streaming data, polyglot environments, performance-sensitive paths.

instruction: Explain HTTP/3 and QUIC. What problems do they solve that HTTP/2 doesn't?
output:
<think>
HTTP/2 problem: head-of-line blocking at TCP level. If one TCP packet is lost, ALL streams on that connection stall. HTTP/3 uses QUIC over UDP: each stream has independent loss recovery. Also: faster connection setup (0-RTT), built-in encryption, better mobile experience (connection migration when IP changes).

This is a fundamental concept that appears frequently in software engineering interviews and real-world system design. Understanding the underlying mechanics helps make better architectural decisions and debug performance issues more effectively. The key is to build intuition through practice with concrete examples rather than memorizing abstract definitions.

instruction: When should I use WebSockets vs Server-Sent Events vs long polling?
output:
<think>
WebSockets: full-duplex, bidirectional, persistent connection. Use for: chat, real-time collaboration, gaming. SSE: server-to-client only, auto-reconnect, works through proxies. Use for: notifications, live feeds, dashboards. Long polling: simulated real-time using repeated HTTP requests. Use for: simple use cases where WebSocket infrastructure is unavailable.

This is a fundamental concept that appears frequently in software engineering interviews and real-world system design. Understanding the underlying mechanics helps make better architectural decisions and debug performance issues more effectively.
